subtopic: Vaginal discharge - microscopy and Amsel recall
stem: A 27-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with two weeks of a thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour, particularly noticeable after intercourse. She has no vulval itch, no dysuria, no abdominal pain, no abnormal bleeding and no fever. She is in a monogamous relationship with a male partner of 18 months, uses a copper IUD for contraception, and her last cervical screening test 14 months ago was normal. On speculum examination there is a thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation. Vaginal pH on narrow-range paper at the lateral wall is 5.2. Addition of 10% potassium hydroxide to a discharge sample produces a strong amine odour.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: thin homogeneous grey-white discharge coating vaginal walls, no inflammation, no strawberry cervix.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.2. KOH whiff test positive. Wet mount: clue cells present, few lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and microscopy picture?

options.text: Bacterial vaginosis
options.rationale: Three of four Amsel criteria are met (thin homogeneous discharge, pH > 4.5, positive amine whiff, clue cells on microscopy), confirming BV per Australasian Sexual Health Alliance and Therapeutic Guidelines.
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

options.text: Vulvovaginal candidiasis
options.rationale: Candidiasis typically presents with vulval itch, thick curdy white discharge, normal pH (< 4.5), pseudohyphae on KOH microscopy and a negative whiff test (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trichomoniasis
options.rationale: Trichomoniasis can raise pH and produce odour but typically shows frothy yellow-green discharge, strawberry cervix and motile flagellated protozoa on wet mount, none of which are present here (ASHA).

options.text: Chlamydia cervicitis
options.rationale: Chlamydia cervicitis is often asymptomatic or produces mucopurulent endocervical discharge with contact bleeding, not a thin homogeneous vaginal discharge with positive amine test (ASHA).

options.text: Physiological discharge
options.rationale: Physiological discharge has pH < 4.5, no amine odour, lactobacilli-predominant flora and no clue cells (Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed clinically by Amsel criteria (3 of 4: thin grey-white discharge, pH > 4.5, positive whiff with KOH, clue cells) or by Nugent score >= 7 on Gram stain. First-line treatment is oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days.
key_points: Amsel: 3 of 4 (discharge, pH > 4.5, whiff, clue cells)
Nugent score >= 7 confirms BV on Gram stain
Candida: itch, curdy, pH < 4.5, pseudohyphae
Trichomonas: frothy yellow-green, strawberry cervix, motile
Lactobacillus loss is the unifying pathophysiology of BV
explanation.pearls: A positive whiff test plus clue cells is virtually pathognomonic. BV is not classified as an STI but is associated with sexual activity and increases HIV/STI acquisition risk.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a 48-hour history of painful vulval ulceration, dysuria so severe she is voiding in the shower, and tender bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. She has had one new male partner over the past two months and has never had similar symptoms before. She is otherwise well, BMI 22, not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), takes the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no immunosuppression and is fully vaccinated. On examination there are multiple shallow tender vesicles and ulcers on bilateral labia minora and the introitus with surrounding erythema, and several smaller vesicles on the perineum. There is no cervical motion tenderness on speculum examination, no abnormal vaginal discharge, and no urinary retention requiring catheterisation at this point. A swab from a deroofed vesicle is sent for HSV PCR and type-specific HSV-1/HSV-2 serology has been requested. Renal function is normal, she has no allergies, and her last sexual health screen six months ago was negative for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis and HIV. She asks how the clinician will treat this presumed first episode while results are pending.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Multiple tender shallow ulcers and intact vesicles on bilateral labia minora and introitus. Tender bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. HSV PCR from vesicle base pending. Type-specific HSV-1/HSV-2 serology pending. Creatinine 62 micromol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antiviral regimen for this first clinical episode of genital herpes?

options.text: Aciclovir 200 mg orally five times daily for 3 days
options.rationale: 200 mg five times daily is a recurrence regimen and the duration is too short for a first episode, which requires 5-10 days of treatment (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes

options.text: Aciclovir 400 mg orally three times daily for 5 to 10 days
options.rationale: Aciclovir 400 mg TDS for 5-10 days (often 7 days, extended to 10 if lesions still forming at day 5) is the recommended first-episode regimen in Australian guidance (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes

options.text: Aciclovir 400 mg orally twice daily continuously as suppressive therapy
options.rationale: 400 mg BD is the suppressive regimen used for frequent recurrences (>= 6 per year) or for partner protection, not for treating an acute first episode (ASHM).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes

options.text: Valaciclovir 500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days
options.rationale: Valaciclovir 500 mg BD for 3 days is a recurrence regimen; first-episode valaciclovir dosing is 500 mg BD for 5-10 days (ASHM).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes

options.text: Topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Topical aciclovir is not effective for genital herpes and is not recommended in Australian or international guidelines; systemic therapy is required (ASHM; BASHH).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes
BASHH UK national guideline - Genital herpes

explanation.summary: First-episode genital herpes is treated with oral aciclovir 400 mg TDS for 5-10 days (or valaciclovir 500 mg BD or famciclovir 250 mg TDS). Treatment is most effective if started within 5 days of onset or while new lesions are forming. Topical antivirals do not work.
key_points: First episode: aciclovir 400 mg TDS x 5-10 days
Recurrence: aciclovir 800 mg TDS x 2 days OR 400 mg TDS x 3-5 days
Suppression: aciclovir 400 mg BD (frequent recurrence or partner protection)
Type HSV PCR plus type-specific serology for counselling
Saline baths, lignocaine 2% gel, simple analgesia
explanation.pearls: HSV-1 increasingly causes genital herpes through oral-genital contact and tends to recur less frequently than HSV-2. Typing predicts recurrence rate and informs counselling.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Genital herpes
BASHH UK national guideline - Genital herpes

stem: A 21-year-old woman presents with multiple soft non-keratinised fleshy lesions on the vulva and perianal area, first noticed six weeks ago. She has had two male partners in the past year and uses depot medroxyprogesterone for contraception. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today) and is otherwise well. She did not receive the HPV vaccine in school. On examination there are approximately 10 discrete papillomatous lesions ranging from 2 to 5 mm distributed over the labia majora and around the anal verge. There are no internal vaginal or cervical lesions on speculum examination and no urethral or intra-anal involvement. Her last cervical screening test was normal six months ago. She prefers a treatment she can apply at home and asks which option is appropriate for self-application.

data_table.Examination: Approximately 10 soft non-keratinised papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm on labia majora and perianal area. No cervical, vaginal, urethral or intra-anal lesions.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Cervical screening test 6 months ago: normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate patient-applied first-line treatment in this woman?

options.text: Podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution applied twice daily for 3 days then 4 days off, for up to 4 cycles
options.rationale: Podophyllotoxin is effective for external non-keratinised warts but is contraindicated in pregnancy and is generally avoided when pregnancy is plausible; imiquimod is the preferred patient-applied option when fertility is a consideration and is the standard ASHA first-line.
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen weekly for 6 weeks
options.rationale: Cryotherapy is clinician-applied, not patient-applied, and is reserved for cases where self-applied therapy is unsuitable or has failed (ASHA).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Imiquimod 5% cream applied three nights per week for up to 16 weeks
options.rationale: Imiquimod 5% cream self-applied three nights weekly (washed off after 6-10 hours) for up to 16 weeks is a recommended patient-applied first-line therapy for external anogenital warts in Australia (ASHA; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Surgical excision under local anaesthetic in a single session
options.rationale: Surgical excision is a clinician-administered modality reserved for large, refractory or keratinised warts; first-line for multiple small external warts is self-applied imiquimod or podophyllotoxin (ASHA).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Trichloroacetic acid 80% applied weekly by the clinician
options.rationale: TCA is clinician-applied (not patient-applied) and is typically reserved for small mucosal lesions or when other options are unsuitable (ASHA; CDC).
source_refs: CDC STI Treatment Guidelines - Anogenital warts

explanation.summary: Patient-applied first-line options for external anogenital warts are imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week up to 16 weeks, or podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution. Clinician-applied options include cryotherapy, TCA and surgical removal. Counsel about HPV vaccination (Gardasil 9 covers HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58) regardless of past exposure.
key_points: Imiquimod 5%: 3 nights/week, wash off after 6-10 h, up to 16 weeks
Podophyllotoxin 0.5%: BD x 3 days then 4 days off, up to 4 cycles; avoid in pregnancy
Cryotherapy / TCA / excision are clinician-applied
HPV vaccine still recommended after warts diagnosis
Recurrence common; warts are not cancer-causing types (6/11)
explanation.pearls: HPV 6 and 11 cause 90% of anogenital warts and are not oncogenic. Cervical screening continues per national programme; warts diagnosis does not change screening intervals.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts
CDC STI Treatment Guidelines - Anogenital warts

stem: A 22-year-old woman attends a university health clinic for an asymptomatic screen after a new sexual relationship over the past three months. She has had three male partners in the past year and uses condoms inconsistently. A self-collected vaginal swab for chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT returns positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae. She has no abdominal or pelvic pain, no abnormal bleeding, no dysuria and no vaginal discharge. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), is on the etonogestrel implant inserted six months ago, has no drug allergies and no history of penicillin reaction. She has never been treated for chlamydia before and her cervical screening test 18 months ago was normal. She is keen to commence treatment today and asks how her recent partners should be managed.

data_table.Examination: Well. No abdominal or pelvic tenderness. Speculum: nil mucopurulent discharge, no contact bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Self-collected vaginal NAAT: Chlamydia trachomatis positive, Neisseria gonorrhoeae negative. Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment regimen for this woman in Australian practice?

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Azithromycin 1 g was previously first-line but doxycycline is now preferred in Australian guidance due to higher cure rates for rectal chlamydia and lower risk of macrolide resistance; azithromycin remains acceptable when adherence to doxycycline is doubtful.

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days is the preferred first-line treatment for uncomplicated genital and rectal chlamydia in Australian guidelines and Therapeutic Guidelines, with higher cure rates than single-dose azithromycin particularly for rectal infection.

options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is reserved for chlamydia in pregnancy when doxycycline and azithromycin are unsuitable; it is not first-line in a non-pregnant adult (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - Chlamydia in pregnancy

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly as a single dose
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone treats gonorrhoea, not chlamydia, and would be inappropriate monotherapy here (ASHM).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 21 days
options.rationale: 21 days is the regimen for lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) caused by C. trachomatis serovars L1-L3, not uncomplicated urogenital chlamydia (ASHM; BASHH).

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated urogenital or rectal chlamydia is treated with doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days (preferred) or azithromycin 1 g single dose (alternative if adherence concerns). Trace partners from the past 6 months. Test of cure is NOT routine for genital infection but IS recommended for rectal infection, pregnancy and persistent symptoms, performed at 4 weeks post-treatment.
key_points: First-line: doxycycline 100 mg BD x 7 days
Alternative: azithromycin 1 g single dose
Pregnancy: azithromycin 1 g (or amoxicillin) - doxycycline contraindicated
Partner notification: 6 months (or last partner if longer)
Test of cure at 4 weeks for rectal, pregnancy, persistent symptoms
explanation.pearls: Re-testing for re-infection (not test of cure) is recommended at 3 months after treatment because re-infection is common.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with five days of yellow vaginal discharge, mild lower abdominal discomfort and intermittent intermenstrual bleeding. She has had two new male partners over the past two months, neither using condoms reliably, and one partner recently disclosed being treated for an STI without specifying which. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), takes the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no drug allergies, and has never had gonorrhoea or chlamydia before. On speculum examination there is mucopurulent endocervical discharge with easily induced contact bleeding from a friable cervix. There is no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal tenderness, no fever and no peritonism. An endocervical swab NAAT is positive for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, and a culture has been sent for antimicrobial susceptibility. She is keen to commence treatment today and the clinic has both intramuscular and oral antibiotics available.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Speculum: mucopurulent endocervical discharge, friable cervix with contact bleeding. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass, no peritonism.
data_table.Investigations: NAAT: N. gonorrhoeae positive, C. trachomatis positive. Gonococcal culture and susceptibility sent. Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical treatment regimen?

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Ciprofloxacin monotherapy is no longer recommended for empirical gonorrhoea treatment in Australia due to widespread quinolone resistance; reserved only if susceptibility is confirmed (ASHM).

options.text: Azithromycin 2 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Azithromycin monotherapy is not recommended due to high rates of macrolide resistance and risk of treatment failure for pharyngeal infection (ASHM).

options.text: Cefixime 400 mg orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Oral cefixime is reserved for situations where IM ceftriaxone is not feasible because of higher MICs and rising oral cephalosporin resistance (ASHM; BASHH).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly as a single dose plus azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus azithromycin 1 g PO is the Australian first-line dual therapy for uncomplicated gonorrhoea; ceftriaxone targets gonorrhoea (with rising MIC concerns) and azithromycin covers presumptive chlamydia plus provides synergy. Doxycycline 100 mg BD x 7 days for chlamydia is an alternative co-therapy.

options.text: Benzathine penicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single dose
options.rationale: Benzathine penicillin treats syphilis and has no role in gonorrhoea, which is intrinsically less susceptible and frequently penicillin-resistant (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated gonorrhoea in Australia is treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus azithromycin 1 g PO single dose (dual therapy); the azithromycin covers co-existing chlamydia and provides synergy against ceftriaxone-resistant strains. Culture and susceptibility must be sent before treatment whenever possible. Test of cure at 2 weeks for pharyngeal infection or any treatment failure.
key_points: Dual therapy: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + azithromycin 1 g PO
Always send culture before treatment for susceptibility
Pharyngeal: TOC at 2 weeks (NAAT) and 4 weeks (culture)
Partner notification: 2 months
Disseminated gonococcal infection: ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily for 7 days
explanation.pearls: Disseminated gonococcal infection presents with the triad of dermatitis, tenosynovitis and migratory polyarthralgia or septic arthritis; blood cultures often negative, but skin lesion or joint aspirate PCR may confirm.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a painless ulcer on the right labia majora first noticed eight days ago. She has had two new sexual contacts over the past three months including one overseas partner. The ulcer is solitary, indurated and clean-based with a rolled edge and is associated with non-tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy. She has no rash, no oral lesions, no fever and no joint pain. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no penicillin allergy, no immunosuppression, and is otherwise well with a BMI of 23. Dark-field microscopy is not available locally but a swab from the ulcer base has been sent for Treponema pallidum PCR. Treponemal (CMIA) serology returns reactive and the RPR titre is 1:32. She has never previously been diagnosed with or treated for syphilis. HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C and gonorrhoea/chlamydia screens have been sent.

data_table.Examination: Solitary 8 mm indurated painless ulcer with clean base and rolled edge on right labia majora. Non-tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy. No palmar/plantar rash, no condylomata lata, no mucous patches.
data_table.Investigations: Treponemal CMIA: reactive. RPR: 1:32. T. pallidum PCR from ulcer pending. Urine beta-hCG negative. HIV, HBV, HCV, gonorrhoea/chlamydia screens pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment regimen for this stage of syphilis?

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Doxycycline 14 days is a second-line option reserved for non-pregnant patients with confirmed penicillin allergy; benzathine penicillin remains first-line when there is no allergy (ASHM).

options.text: Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) intramuscularly as a single dose
options.rationale: A single dose of benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) IM is the first-line treatment for primary, secondary and early latent syphilis (acquired within the past 2 years) in Australian guidance (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly weekly for three doses
options.rationale: Three weekly doses are required for late latent syphilis or syphilis of unknown duration, not for primary syphilis acquired within the past 2 years (ASHM).

options.text: Procaine penicillin 1.5 g intramuscularly daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Procaine penicillin daily for 10-15 days is used for neurosyphilis or ocular/otic syphilis when IV benzylpenicillin is not feasible, not for primary syphilis (ASHM).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g intramuscularly daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is an alternative when penicillin is not available; first-line for primary syphilis without allergy is single-dose benzathine penicillin (CDC; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Primary syphilis (painless indurated chancre, regional lymphadenopathy, RPR reactive, treponemal serology reactive, acquired within 2 years) is treated with a single dose of benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) IM. Late latent or unknown-duration syphilis requires 3 weekly doses. Counsel about Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, chills, myalgia 4-12 h after first dose) and notify partners (3 months for primary, 6 months for secondary, 12 months for early latent).
key_points: Primary chancre: painless, indurated, single, clean base
Primary/secondary/early latent: single benzathine 1.8 g IM
Late latent or unknown duration: 3 weekly IM doses
Neurosyphilis/ocular/otic: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly x 15 days
Jarisch-Herxheimer common 4-12 h after first dose
explanation.pearls: Adequate response = 4-fold (2-dilution) drop in RPR titre by 6-12 months. Persistent or rising titres indicate treatment failure or reinfection. Always screen for co-existing HIV.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with five days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, intermenstrual bleeding and a low-grade fever. She has had one new sexual partner over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L), has no allergies, takes only the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no recent gynaecological instrumentation and no IUD in situ. She has not had previous PID. On examination her temperature is 37.9 degrees C, abdomen is mildly tender suprapubically without peritonism, bowel sounds are normal, and on bimanual examination there is bilateral adnexal tenderness with marked cervical motion tenderness. Speculum reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge. Endocervical NAATs and gonococcal culture have been sent. There is no palpable adnexal mass, no fluctuant cul-de-sac mass on examination, and the patient tolerates oral fluids and analgesia. The treating registrar judges her safe for outpatient management with close 48-hour review.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Suprapubic tenderness without peritonism. Speculum: mucopurulent cervical discharge. Bimanual: marked cervical motion tenderness, bilateral adnexal tenderness, no mass.
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. Endocervical NAAT for gonorrhoea/chlamydia and culture sent. WCC 12.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 48 mg/L. Pelvic ultrasound: no tubo-ovarian abscess.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly stat, then doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines and Australasian STI Guidelines recommend ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat (to cover gonorrhoea) plus 14 days of doxycycline 100 mg BD (chlamydia and atypical organisms) plus metronidazole 400 mg BD (anaerobes including BV-associated organisms) for outpatient PID.

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate does not provide reliable cover for Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Chlamydia trachomatis and is not recommended for PID (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Doxycycline alone covers chlamydia but does not adequately cover gonorrhoea or anaerobes; PID requires combination therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Azithromycin 1 g orally weekly for 2 weeks
options.rationale: Single-agent azithromycin is inadequate for PID and risks treatment failure and antimicrobial resistance (Therapeutic Guidelines; BASHH).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously daily plus metronidazole 500 mg intravenously twice daily until afebrile for 24 hours
options.rationale: IV therapy is reserved for inpatient PID with tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, severe sepsis, failed oral therapy, or inability to tolerate oral intake; this patient is suitable for outpatient management (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Outpatient PID in Australia is treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Admit for IV therapy if pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess, severe sepsis, inability to tolerate oral intake, surgical abdomen needing exclusion, or failure of outpatient therapy at 48-72 hours. Review at 48-72 hours mandatory.
key_points: Empirical Rx: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + doxycycline 100 mg BD x 14 d + metronidazole 400 mg BD x 14 d
Admit if: pregnant, TOA, severe sepsis, vomiting, surgical abdomen, failed oral
48-72 h review essential
Treat partners empirically (last 6 months)
IUD does not need removal unless no improvement at 72 h
explanation.pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis - have a low threshold to treat young sexually active women with pelvic pain plus cervical motion or adnexal tenderness, as missed PID is a leading cause of tubal infertility and ectopic pregnancy.

subtopic: PID complication - right upper quadrant pain
stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder that has worsened over the past 36 hours, with a pleuritic component that is worse on deep inspiration and on lying flat. She also reports four days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and intermenstrual bleeding. She has had two new male partners over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L today), has no significant medical history, has no history of gallstones or biliary disease, drinks 4 standard drinks per week, and is on no regular medications other than the etonogestrel implant. On examination her temperature is 38.1 degrees C, pulse 104 regular, blood pressure 116/72, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99% on room air. There is right upper quadrant tenderness without Murphy's sign, mild generalised lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism, and bilateral adnexal tenderness with cervical motion tenderness on bimanual examination. Speculum shows mucopurulent cervical discharge with contact bleeding. Liver function tests show mild transaminitis (ALT 78 U/L, AST 62 U/L) with normal bilirubin and ALP. Right upper quadrant ultrasound shows normal gallbladder and biliary tree with no stones, no wall thickening and no pericholecystic fluid. Pelvic ultrasound shows no tubo-ovarian abscess but trace pelvic free fluid. Endocervical NAAT is positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 38.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: RUQ tender, Murphy's negative. Lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism. CMT, bilateral adnexal tenderness. Mucopurulent cervical discharge.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. ALT 78, AST 62, bilirubin 14, ALP 92. RUQ US: normal GB, no stones. Pelvic US: no TOA, trace free fluid. NAAT: C. trachomatis positive.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis explaining the right upper quadrant pain?

options.text: Acute viral hepatitis
options.rationale: Mild transaminitis with concurrent PID and chlamydia is far more consistent with perihepatitis; viral hepatitis would not explain CMT, mucopurulent cervicitis or RUQ pleuritic pain and should produce more marked aminotransferase elevation (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - Acute viral hepatitis

options.text: Acute cholecystitis
options.rationale: RUQ ultrasound is normal with no stones, no wall thickening, no pericholecystic fluid and Murphy's sign is negative, making acute cholecystitis very unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Tubo-ovarian abscess with subdiaphragmatic spread
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound did not show a tubo-ovarian abscess; trace pelvic free fluid alone does not constitute a TOA, and a TOA would not typically present primarily with RUQ pain (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
options.rationale: Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L, excluding ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (gonococcal or chlamydial perihepatitis)
options.rationale: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis is perihepatitis from haematogenous or transperitoneal spread of chlamydia (or gonorrhoea) complicating PID. RUQ pain with mild transaminitis, normal biliary imaging and concurrent PID with positive chlamydia NAAT fits the classical picture (ASHM; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is perihepatitis complicating PID, classically due to Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae, producing RUQ pain with characteristic 'violin-string' adhesions between liver capsule and abdominal wall at laparoscopy. Mild transaminitis is common; biliary imaging is normal. Treatment is the same as for PID (ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole for 14 days).
key_points: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis = chlamydial/gonococcal perihepatitis
RUQ pain + PID + normal biliary tree
Violin-string adhesions on laparoscopy (classical)
Treat as PID (no different regimen)
Other PID complications: TOA, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, 6-10x ectopic risk
explanation.pearls: Suspect Fitz-Hugh-Curtis in any sexually active young woman with RUQ pain and pelvic findings, even when LFTs are only mildly deranged. It is a clinical and supportive imaging diagnosis - laparoscopy is rarely performed.

subtopic: HIV in pregnancy - mode of delivery decision
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with chronic HIV-1 infection is reviewed at 36+2 weeks gestation in the antenatal HIV clinic to plan delivery. She has been on tenofovir/emtricitabine/dolutegravir throughout this pregnancy, commenced at 9 weeks gestation, and her adherence has been excellent on pharmacy refill audit. Her booking CD4 count was 540 cells/microL with a baseline HIV viral load of 8400 copies/mL prior to recommencing therapy. Her most recent HIV viral load taken at 36+0 weeks is < 50 copies/mL (suppressed) and her last three viral loads in the third trimester (at 28, 32 and 36 weeks) have all been < 50 copies/mL. She had a normal vaginal birth with her previous pregnancy four years ago when she was already on suppressive cART. She has no obstetric contraindication to vaginal birth, the fetus is cephalic with appropriate interval growth on the 50th centile, there is no placenta praevia on the 32-week scan, and she has not had any complications this pregnancy. Her hepatitis B and hepatitis C serology are negative, syphilis serology is negative, and the 36-week GBS swab is negative. She is asking about the recommended mode of delivery, whether she requires intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, whether her baby will require post-exposure prophylaxis, and whether she can breastfeed.

data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 36 cm. Cephalic presentation. No oedema.
data_table.Investigations: HIV viral load at 36+0: < 50 copies/mL (suppressed). CD4 540 cells/microL. HBV/HCV negative. GBS swab pending. Growth scan 34 weeks: 50th centile, normal Dopplers.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for mode of delivery and neonatal prophylaxis?

options.text: Planned vaginal birth, no intrapartum zidovudine required, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: Mode of delivery and neonatal PEP regimen are correct in spirit, but neonatal zidovudine is given for 4 weeks (in low-risk infants per Australian guidance) rather than the variable 2-4 week phrasing; the more precise duration is 4 weeks of neonatal zidovudine.

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine for 6 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: With a suppressed viral load, vaginal birth is preferred over caesarean; the maternal cART has effectively eliminated transmission risk and CS adds maternal morbidity without benefit (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 38+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal triple-drug PEP for 4 weeks, breastfeeding permitted if exclusive
options.rationale: Planned CS is reserved for viral load >= 50-1000 copies/mL near term; IV zidovudine and triple-drug neonatal PEP apply to higher-risk scenarios; breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia regardless of viral suppression (ASHM).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth at term, no intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: When maternal HIV viral load is < 50 copies/mL near term, planned vaginal birth is recommended; intravenous intrapartum zidovudine is NOT required for suppressed viral load; the neonate receives oral zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks; breastfeeding is not recommended in high-income settings where safe formula is available (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth with intrapartum intravenous zidovudine infusion regardless of viral load, neonatal nevirapine single dose only
options.rationale: IV intrapartum zidovudine is only required for unsuppressed viral load (>= 50-1000) or unknown viral load at delivery; neonatal single-dose nevirapine alone is inadequate and not the Australian standard (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Women on cART with viral load < 50 copies/mL near term can safely have a planned vaginal birth; intrapartum IV zidovudine is not required. Neonate receives 4 weeks of oral zidovudine monotherapy (low-risk). Formula feeding is recommended in Australia. Planned caesarean at 38-39 weeks is reserved for viral load >= 50-1000 (or unknown) near term, with IV zidovudine intrapartum and consideration of neonatal triple therapy.
key_points: VL < 50 at 36 weeks: vaginal birth, no IV zidovudine, neonatal AZT x 4 weeks
VL 50-1000: individualised; consider CS at 38-39 weeks
VL > 1000 or unknown: pre-labour CS at 38-39 weeks + IV zidovudine + neonatal triple PEP
Avoid invasive monitoring (FSE, FBS) if possible during labour
Formula feeding recommended in Australia (no breastfeeding)
explanation.pearls: Vertical transmission risk falls to < 1% (and approaches 0.1%) when maternal viral load is suppressed throughout pregnancy. The biggest modifiable factor is maternal adherence, not mode of delivery.

stem: A 26-year-old woman attends the emergency department 28 hours after condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a male partner she has just learned is living with HIV-1. He told her his combination antiretroviral treatment was 'on and off' over the past six months because he had run out of his prescription, and she does not know his current viral load. She has no symptoms, no genital ulceration, no menstruation at the time of exposure, the partner does not inject drugs, and there was no concurrent intercourse with other partners. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no significant past medical history, is on no regular medications other than the etonogestrel implant, has no drug allergies, has normal renal function on baseline pathology (creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90), is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated as a teenager), HIV antibody/antigen negative on today's baseline 4th-generation test, hepatitis C antibody negative, and syphilis EIA negative. She is asking what medications she should take, how long she needs to take them for, when she will be re-tested for HIV, and what side effects she should monitor for. She is not breastfeeding and has no plans for pregnancy in the next month.

data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab negative. HBsAb positive (vaccinated). HCV Ab negative. Syphilis EIA negative. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis regimen and follow-up plan?

options.text: Single-dose tenofovir 300 mg, repeat HIV testing at 6 weeks
options.rationale: PEP requires a 28-day course of combination antiretrovirals, not a single dose or monotherapy, which would not prevent HIV acquisition (ASHM PEP guidelines).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg daily for 28 days, no third agent required
options.rationale: Dual NRTI-only PEP is no longer recommended in Australia; current guidance is three-drug PEP (typically TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir or raltegravir) for high-risk exposures (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir alafenamide 25 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg plus rilpivirine 25 mg daily for 7 days
options.rationale: PEP duration is 28 days, not 7 days; rilpivirine is not a standard PEP third agent due to interactions and resistance considerations (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg daily plus dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days, HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks and 3 months
options.rationale: Australian PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a source person with HIV not on effective treatment, within 72 hours) is TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks, and 3 months post-exposure (ASHM Australian PEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PEP until source partner viral load is known, retest at 4 weeks only
options.rationale: PEP efficacy falls rapidly with time and must be commenced within 72 hours (ideally < 24 hours); delaying for source results risks losing the treatment window (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Non-occupational HIV PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive anal/vaginal sex with a source with HIV who is not virally suppressed, within 72 hours) is TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg + dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days. Baseline tests: HIV Ag/Ab, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, renal function. Follow-up HIV tests at 6 weeks and 3 months (4th gen). Counsel on adherence, side effects, and condom use during follow-up.
key_points: Window: < 72 hours, ideally < 24 hours
Regimen: TDF/FTC + dolutegravir (or raltegravir) x 28 days
Baseline: HIV, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, U&E
Follow-up: HIV Ag/Ab at 6 weeks and 3 months
If source has confirmed undetectable VL, PEP may not be needed (U=U)
explanation.pearls: U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): a source with confirmed sustained viral load < 200 copies/mL transmits HIV through sex effectively at zero risk - in those cases, PEP is not required.

subtopic: HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis - eligibility and monitoring
stem: A 29-year-old cisgender woman attends a sexual health service requesting HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. Her current male partner of three months has HIV-1 with a viral load that he reports has been detectable on most recent testing because of treatment interruption while changing jobs. They have condomless vaginal sex 2-3 times per week. She has no symptoms of acute HIV illness in the past 6 weeks (no fever, rash, lymphadenopathy or sore throat), is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), is on the etonogestrel implant for contraception, has no history of renal disease, and is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated). She has not injected drugs. Her last STI screen 8 months ago was negative. She has no allergies and no regular medications other than the implant. She is asking whether she is eligible for PrEP, what baseline tests are required, and what monitoring she will need on therapy.

data_table.Examination: Well. No mucosal lesions, no rash, no lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. HBsAg, anti-HBc and HBsAb pending today. HIV Ag/Ab pending today.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for PrEP initiation and monitoring?

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg only after a 4-week negative HIV window-period repeat test
options.rationale: A single recent negative HIV test plus absence of acute HIV symptoms is sufficient to start PrEP; routine 4-week deferral is not required and would prolong an unnecessary risk window (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Prescribe on-demand (2+1+1) tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine, suitable for all genders
options.rationale: On-demand 2+1+1 dosing is recommended only for cisgender MSM with infrequent exposures; daily PrEP is required for cisgender women because of slower tissue drug levels in cervicovaginal tissue (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg with baseline HIV/HBV/renal screen, then HIV testing and STI screen every 3 months
options.rationale: Daily oral TDF/FTC is the recommended PrEP regimen for cisgender women at substantial risk. Baseline workup includes HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis serology, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT, renal function and beta-hCG. Quarterly monitoring includes HIV testing and STI screen; renal function 6-monthly (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PrEP and recommend condom use only, as PrEP is contraindicated when the partner has detectable viral load
options.rationale: A partner with HIV who has detectable viral load is precisely the scenario in which PrEP provides the greatest benefit; PrEP is not contraindicated and is strongly indicated here (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily dolutegravir 50 mg monotherapy as PrEP
options.rationale: Dolutegravir is not a PrEP agent; PrEP regimens are TDF/FTC (daily) or, for selected MSM, TDF/FTC on-demand (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Cisgender women eligible for PrEP receive daily oral TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg. Baseline workup: HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT (multi-site), renal function, beta-hCG. Quarterly: HIV test, STI screen, adherence and side-effect review. Renal function 6-monthly. On-demand (2+1+1) dosing only for cisgender MSM with predictable infrequent exposures. PrEP can be continued in pregnancy if substantial risk persists.
key_points: Daily TDF/FTC for cisgender women (no on-demand)
MSM may use daily or on-demand (2+1+1)
Baseline: HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis, GC/CT, renal, beta-hCG
Monitoring: HIV + STI every 3 months; renal every 6 months
HBV must be screened pre-PrEP (rebound on cessation)
explanation.pearls: Exclude acute HIV before initiation: ask about fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, sore throat in the past 6 weeks. Acute HIV may have a still-negative Ag/Ab and starting PrEP could select for resistance.

subtopic: Acute sexual assault - forensic and medical priorities
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department at 0300 hours, brought by police, reporting that she was sexually assaulted approximately 14 hours ago at a private residence by an unknown male. The reported assault involved vaginal penetration without a condom, and she believes ejaculation occurred. She has showered once since the assault but has not changed her underwear, brushed her teeth, eaten or passed urine since the event. She has no significant medical history, is on no regular medications, has no drug allergies, has had a normal menstrual period that finished 8 days ago, and is sexually active with a regular partner from whom she uses no contraception. She has had her three-dose HPV vaccine course as a teenager but does not know her hepatitis B vaccination status and does not recall having received the catch-up adolescent dose. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative, has minor bruising on her right upper arm and a small abrasion on her left knee, and has not previously had a forensic examination. There is no immediate life-threatening injury, no head injury, no loss of consciousness, no respiratory or haemodynamic compromise, and she has not consumed alcohol or other substances in the past 24 hours. A specially trained forensic medical examiner is available on call at the local sexual assault service, with chain-of-custody documentation procedures in place, and a social worker can be paged. She is asking what will happen next, what medications she can be offered tonight to reduce pregnancy and infection risk, and whether the examination is mandatory. She has not yet decided whether to formally report to police and is uncertain about disclosing to her family.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Alert, distressed, cooperative. Bruise 3 cm on right upper arm. No external genital injury visible without colposcopic examination.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab, HBsAg/Ab, HCV Ab, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT and trichomonas NAAT sent. Pregnancy and STI follow-up planned per local sexual assault service protocol.

lead_in: Which set of medical and forensic actions is the most appropriate tonight?

options.text: Defer forensic examination until daylight to reduce patient distress, commence empirical STI prophylaxis, and offer emergency contraception
options.rationale: Forensic evidence has a < 72-hour window with degradation over time; delaying examination risks loss of evidence and reduces forensic yield. The examination should be offered now by a trained examiner (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Proceed only with the forensic examination tonight, defer STI prophylaxis and emergency contraception until after the kit is processed
options.rationale: Medical priorities (emergency contraception, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment, hepatitis B vaccination/HBIG, safety planning) must occur alongside forensic examination, not be deferred for kit processing (RANZCOG).

options.text: Forensic examination can proceed only if the patient agrees to formally report the assault to police
options.rationale: Forensic examination should be offered regardless of reporting intent; many services hold kits anonymously to preserve future reporting options. Linking forensic care to reporting acts as a barrier to disclosure (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Forensic examination is contraindicated because the patient has showered; provide reassurance and discharge with general practice follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Showering reduces but does not eliminate forensic yield; trace DNA and other evidence may still be recoverable, and discharge without addressing EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP, HBV and safety is unsafe (RANZCOG).

options.text: Offer immediate forensic medical examination by the trained examiner with chain-of-custody documentation; offer levonorgestrel or ulipristal emergency contraception or copper IUD; offer STI prophylaxis (ceftriaxone IM, azithromycin or doxycycline, metronidazole); assess HIV PEP eligibility; offer hepatitis B vaccination (and HBIG if indicated); arrange safety planning, follow-up and counselling
options.rationale: Acute sexual assault management within 72 hours requires concurrent attention to medical priorities (EC within 120 hours, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment within 72 hours, HBV protection, mental health support and safety planning) and forensic priorities (kit collection with chain of custody by a trained examiner), regardless of whether the patient has decided to report (RANZCOG; ASHM; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

explanation.summary: Acute presentation within 72 hours of sexual assault requires concurrent medical and forensic priorities: forensic examination with chain of custody by a trained examiner regardless of reporting intent; emergency contraception (levonorgestrel up to 96 hours, ulipristal up to 120 hours, or copper IUD up to 5 days); STI baseline tests and prophylaxis (ceftriaxone 500 mg IM, azithromycin 1 g or doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days, metronidazole 2 g single dose); HIV PEP eligibility assessment for source with HIV or unknown source with high-risk features; hepatitis B vaccination and HBIG where indicated; safety planning; mental health support and follow-up at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months.
key_points: Forensic kit window: 72 hours (some up to 7 days for specific samples)
EC: levonorgestrel < 96 h, ulipristal < 120 h, Cu-IUD < 5 days
STI prophylaxis: ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline + metronidazole
HIV PEP: assess within 72 h; high-risk sources warrant PEP
HBV: vaccinate if unvaccinated; HBIG if source HBsAg-positive
Forensic exam offered regardless of reporting intent
explanation.pearls: Documentation matters - use trained examiners (FAMU/SARC), follow chain-of-custody, photograph injuries, store kit per jurisdiction protocol. Mental-health and safety planning are not afterthoughts; ongoing risk of further violence is high in the first weeks.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left iliac fossa pain that began two hours ago while exercising, associated with one episode of vomiting. The pain is colicky on a background of constant severe pain and radiates to the left flank. Her last menstrual period was 35 days ago and she normally has a 28-day cycle, but she has had irregular cycles in the past. She has had one sexual partner over the past year, uses no contraception and has had no recent abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge. She has no fever, no dysuria, no diarrhoea, no anorexia and no migratory periumbilical pain. On examination temperature is 36.9 degrees C, pulse 104, BP 116/72. The abdomen is tender in the left iliac fossa with voluntary guarding but no rebound or percussion tenderness; right iliac fossa is non-tender. Bimanual examination reveals a tender left adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness and the right adnexa is non-tender. Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bedside ultrasound by the registrar shows a 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow and reduced arterial flow.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Left iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no rebound. Right iliac fossa non-tender. Bimanual: tender left adnexal fullness, no cervical motion tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocyte esterase negative. Bedside US: 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow, reduced arterial flow.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Left ovarian torsion
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pain with vomiting on a background of a 7 cm ovarian cyst, tender adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness, negative beta-hCG, and ultrasound showing absent venous and reduced arterial Doppler flow are classical for ovarian torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L, definitively excluding ectopic pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: PID typically presents with bilateral pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge and fever, often with subacute onset over days. The acute unilateral picture with abnormal Doppler on a cyst fits torsion better (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Acute appendicitis
options.rationale: Appendicitis classically produces migratory periumbilical-to-right-iliac-fossa pain with anorexia and low-grade fever; left iliac fossa pain with adnexal Doppler abnormalities makes this unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Renal colic from left ureteric stone
options.rationale: Renal colic typically produces flank-to-groin pain with microscopic haematuria; urinalysis shows no blood and ultrasound localises the abnormality to the ovary with abnormal Doppler, making torsion far more likely (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Classical features: sudden unilateral pelvic pain, often during physical activity, with nausea and vomiting; tender adnexal mass on bimanual examination; ovarian cyst (typically > 5 cm) on imaging; reduced or absent Doppler flow (venous flow lost first, then arterial). Diagnosis often requires diagnostic laparoscopy as Doppler can be normal in intermittent torsion. Detorsion with cystectomy is preferred over oophorectomy when ovarian tissue appears viable.
key_points: Torsion: sudden unilateral pain, vomiting, cyst > 5 cm, abnormal Doppler
Ectopic: beta-hCG positive, missed period, PV bleed, hypovolaemia if ruptured
PID: bilateral pain, fever, CMT, mucopurulent discharge, days-onset
Appendicitis: migratory pain, anorexia, RIF, McBurney's, Rovsing's
Doppler: venous flow lost first; intermittent torsion may show preserved flow
explanation.pearls: Beta-hCG must be checked in every woman of reproductive age with pelvic pain; missing ectopic is a never-event. A normal Doppler does not exclude torsion - if clinical suspicion is high, proceed to diagnostic laparoscopy.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 18+3 weeks gestation presents to her midwife with two weeks of a thin off-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour. She has no abdominal pain, no abnormal bleeding, no fever, no contractions and no fluid leakage. She is otherwise well, BMI at booking 25, no medical comorbidities, no previous preterm birth (the first pregnancy delivered at 39+2), no known cervical insufficiency, and the dating scan at 9 weeks confirmed a singleton intrauterine pregnancy. Her booking bloods including HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B and rubella serology were negative or immune. Speculum examination shows a thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation, a closed cervix without lesions, and no liquor pooling. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall is 5.1 and addition of 10% potassium hydroxide produces a strong amine odour. Wet mount microscopy shows clue cells and few lactobacilli with no motile trichomonads and no pseudohyphae.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge, closed cervix, no liquor pooling. No external genital lesions.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.1. KOH whiff test positive. Wet mount: clue cells present, few lactobacilli, no trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. Booking HIV, syphilis, HBV negative; rubella immune.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment for this woman's bacterial vaginosis at 18+3 weeks gestation?

options.text: No treatment required; bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is self-limiting and treatment does not reduce preterm birth risk
options.rationale: Symptomatic BV warrants treatment in pregnancy for symptom relief regardless of preterm birth evidence; current Australian guidance treats symptomatic BV in pregnancy with oral metronidazole (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream nightly for 7 nights
options.rationale: Clindamycin vaginal cream is avoided in the second half of pregnancy due to oil-based vehicle weakening latex and concerns about preterm birth and neonatal outcomes; oral therapy is preferred in pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Metronidazole 400 mg orally twice daily for 5 to 7 days
options.rationale: Oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days is the first-line treatment for symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy beyond the first trimester in Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Tinidazole is not recommended in pregnancy due to limited safety data and is contraindicated in the first trimester; oral metronidazole has the established pregnancy safety profile (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

options.text: Metronidazole 2 g orally as a single dose
options.rationale: Single-dose 2 g metronidazole has lower cure rates than the 5-7 day regimen for BV and is generally not first-line in pregnancy, where a longer course is preferred (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is treated with oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days. Clindamycin vaginal cream is avoided in pregnancy (oil-based, weakens latex condoms, theoretical association with adverse outcomes). Diagnosis is by Amsel criteria (3 of 4) or Nugent score >= 7. Screening of asymptomatic women is not recommended (no benefit in low-risk women; mixed evidence in high-risk).
key_points: Treatment of choice in pregnancy: oral metronidazole 400 mg BD x 5-7 days
Single-dose 2 g and tinidazole both inferior choices in pregnancy
Avoid clindamycin vaginal cream (oil-based, weakens latex)
Amsel 3 of 4 or Nugent >= 7 for diagnosis
Routine asymptomatic screening not recommended
explanation.pearls: BV associates with preterm birth but treatment in low-risk asymptomatic women does not consistently reduce preterm birth; treat symptomatic women for symptom relief and individualise in those with previous preterm birth.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic - BV in pregnancy
Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

stem: A 32-year-old non-pregnant woman presents to her general practice with three weeks of malodorous frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulval irritation, dyspareunia and post-coital spotting. She has had two new male partners over the past four months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), is on the depot medroxyprogesterone injection for contraception, has no drug allergies, drinks 6 standard drinks per week socially, and has not been treated for an STI before. She has had no recent antibiotic use and her last cervical screening test 14 months ago was normal. On speculum examination the cervix is friable with multiple punctate haemorrhages giving a strawberry appearance, and there is copious frothy yellow-green discharge pooling in the vagina with a strong amine odour on KOH. Vaginal pH on lateral wall paper is 5.6 and saline wet-mount microscopy shows motile flagellated protozoa moving across the field. There is no cervical motion tenderness or adnexal tenderness on bimanual examination.

data_table.Examination: Strawberry cervix with punctate haemorrhages. Copious frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.6. Saline wet mount: motile flagellated protozoa. Trichomonas NAAT pending. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment regimen?

options.text: Clotrimazole 500 mg pessary single dose
options.rationale: Clotrimazole treats vulvovaginal candidiasis, not Trichomonas vaginalis; nitroimidazole therapy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel intravaginally for 5 nights
options.rationale: Topical metronidazole does not achieve adequate concentrations at extravaginal reservoirs (urethra, Skene's glands) and has higher failure rates; oral therapy is required (CDC; ASHM).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days without partner treatment
options.rationale: Doxycycline does not treat trichomonas and partner treatment is required to prevent reinfection (ASHM).

options.text: Metronidazole 400 mg orally twice daily for 5 days with no partner treatment
options.rationale: Duration is too short (7 days standard for BD regimen) and partner treatment is essential; omitting partner therapy causes reinfection cycles (ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Metronidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with empirical treatment of recent male sexual partners
options.rationale: Metronidazole 2 g single dose (or 400 mg BD for 7 days) is first-line for trichomoniasis in non-pregnant adults; partner notification and empirical treatment are essential to prevent reinfection (Australasian STI Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Trichomoniasis in non-pregnant adults is treated with oral metronidazole 2 g single dose, or 400 mg BD for 7 days (alternative for those at higher risk of treatment failure such as HIV). In pregnancy, treatment is still recommended (oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days). Always treat sexual partners empirically. Test of cure not routine but re-test for reinfection at 3 months.
key_points: First-line: metronidazole 2 g PO stat OR 400 mg BD x 7 d
Strawberry cervix (colpitis macularis) in 2-5% but pathognomonic when present
Motile flagellated protozoa on wet mount confirms; NAAT most sensitive
Partner notification and empirical treatment essential
Pregnancy: treat with oral metronidazole; avoid topical preparations
explanation.pearls: Avoid alcohol during and for 24 hours after metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). HIV co-infection increases treatment failure risk; consider 7-day BD regimen in that group.
